qa_pair.question: Among the courses you completed BEFORE the current semester (i.e. courses still in your enrollment list but no longer active for grading), identify the one whose most recent announcement was authored as a "Welcome / orientation" message at the start of that course's semester. Reply with the exact title of that announcement.
qa_pair.answer: [FILL_IN: announcement title string, e.g. "Welcome to AY2024-25 Sem 1"]

qa_pair.question: Look at all gradebook columns across your active courses this semester. Find the column with the SINGLE HIGHEST "possible" (maximum) score. Reply with the displayName of that column.

In any course you have taken, find a lesson or content folder whose title (or description) discusses the concept of recursion (do not search the literal word — search related terms such as "self-referential", "divide and conquer", "induction"). Among matches, pick the one whose breadcrumb path is shortest (fewest intermediate folders). Reply with the title of the matched item.

For the most recently accessed course in your enrollment list, page through its announcements and locate the first one (oldest, by creation timestamp) that explicitly references a specific date format such as a deadline. Reply with the calendar month and year that announcement was created, formatted as YYYY-MM.

Pick any one course you completed at least one semester ago. Open its top-level content tree and recursively walk into folders until you find a PDF lecture slide deck. Read the contents of that PDF. Reply with the number of pages reported by read_file_content's pageCount field for the FIRST PDF you encounter when walking children in the order returned by the API (depth-first, in array order).

Find a course whose syllabus or course outline document, when read inline, contains a textbook reference. Reply with the surname (last name) of the textbook's first author as printed in that document. If multiple courses match, pick the one with the alphabetically earliest course title.
Among your active courses, which one has the LARGEST number of gradebook columns where availability is "Yes"? Reply with the course's title (the title field returned by list_courses).

Search every active course for content items whose title or description mentions group work or team projects (do NOT search literal "group" — use synonyms: "collaborative", "peer", "team-based"). Among the matches, find the one with the deepest breadcrumb (most levels of nesting). Reply with the course title (NOT content title) of that match.

Look at announcements in the course you most recently accessed. Among the three most recent announcements, identify the one with the longest body text (by character count of rawText). Reply with TRUE if that announcement was modified after creation, FALSE if not.

Pick a course whose content tree has at least one folder titled with a week number (e.g., "Week 1", "Week 5"). Open the highest-numbered such week folder and use read_file_content on the first content item with attached files. Among the files returned, count how many were SKIPPED (returned in the `skipped` array, not the `files` array). Reply with that integer count. If your highest-numbered week folder contains no items with attached files, fall back to the next-highest numbered week.
